previewTitle.value: From the Andes to the Atlantic

title.value: From the Andes to the Atlantic

subtitle.value: The Rolex and National Geographic perpetual planet Amazon expedition

text.value: Renowned National Geographic Explorer and photographer Thomas Peschak has spent a lifetime documenting our vast oceans and the beauty of the marine environment. Over the past year, with the support of the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative and National Geographic Society, he has been diving into the freshwater world of the Amazon Rainforest.

title.value: EXPLORING THE HEART OF THE PLANET

text.value:
In April 2022, the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative and the National Geographic Society launched the Perpetual Planet Amazon Expedition, a two-year series of scientific studies to cover the Amazon River basin. As the “Heart of the Planet”, the area is rich in biodiversity, home to 3 million species of plants and animals, as well as 40 million people. Its system of rivers channels the largest volume of rainfall on Earth. Through its hydrological cycle, the Amazon River and its tributaries not only feed the landscape, but also affect the entire planet.

Award-winning wildlife photographer and National Geographic Explorer Thomas Peschak has been following seven teams of National Geographic Explorers and multi-disciplinary scientists, who are studying the Amazon River basin as part of the Rolex and National Geographic Perpetual Planet Amazon Expedition. They have been working with the local and indigenous communities who call it home to understand its complex aquatic ecosystems and its role as a central lifeforce for the planet.

Beginning his career as a marine biologist, Peschak turned to photojournalism when he recognized the impact of stories and images in conveying messages and evoking empathy. He uses engaging photography to translate scientific findings to a wider audience, making him the perfect candidate to tell the story of the Amazon basin. Drawing on his background documenting marine wildlife, he is now going below the canopy of the rainforest. “Instead of encountering the sharks, whales and sea turtles that I’m used to, I’ll be diving with anacondas, piranhas, and sloths,” he says.

Generally, rainforests have dominated conversations surrounding the Amazon, though deforestation does not affect only the treeline. The aquatic ecosystem is the lifeline of the forest, and the teams, which consist of experts from a range of fields – including ecology, biology, hydrology, climatology, geology and geochemistry – are shifting the focus to the region’s waterways.

title.value: The teams will study sites from the top of the Andes to the river’s mouth at the Atlantic Ocean. They will answer the biggest scientific questions about how the water of the rainforest is being affected by climate change, deforestation, and biodiversity loss.

text.value: As Peschak says, “From the snowfall on Peruvian glaciers to the fisheries that feed people across the entire river basin, the teams will study sites from the top of the Andes to the river’s mouth at the Atlantic Ocean. They will answer the biggest scientific questions about how the water of the rainforest is being affected by climate change, deforestation, and biodiversity loss.”

title.value: THE EXPEDITION SO FAR: RESEARCH & RESULTS

text.value:
Just a year in, the teams of the expedition have been charging ahead on their projects, and many have already started producing results.

National Geographic Explorers Baker Perry and Tom Matthews have installed the highest weather station in the tropical Andes at 6,349 metres, just 35 metres from the peak of Nevado Ausangate, the highest mountain in the tropical Andes, which acts as a water source for much of the Amazon. The station has been providing valuable real-time data on weather patterns over the Amazon, contributing to an understanding of how climate change is affecting the region, and how changes in the region might affect the global climate: “The Amazon is one of the most important regions globally for influencing atmospheric circulation, especially of water,” explains Perry. “It makes us aware of just how significant the Amazon’s impact is, from ecosystems to people to glaciers, well beyond South America.”

title.value: It makes us aware of just how significant the Amazon’s impact is, from ecosystems to people to glaciers, well beyond South America.

text.value: But they are not the only ones to have enjoyed early success. The expeditions have been packed with discoveries and world firsts. National Geographic Explorer Angelo Bernardino produced the first documentation of freshwater mangroves; Rolex Awards for Enterprise Laureate João Campos-Silva and fellow National Geographic Explorer Andressa Scabin became the first people to “GPS (global positioning system) tag” an arapaima, the world’s largest scaled freshwater fish; and National Geographic Explorer Fernando Trujillo has surveyed 1,270 kilometres of rivers across four countries, calculating there to be 1,366 of the iconic pink Amazon river dolphins in the region. Furthermore, National Geographic Explorer Thiago Silva is creating the first three-dimensional model of the river’s floodplains, with the aim of creating an interactive environment that people can visit and explore online for themselves.

legendRoller.value: National Geographic Explorer Angelo Bernardino produced the first documentation of freshwater mangroves.

Rolex Awards for Enterprise Laureate João Campos-Silva and fellow National Geographic Explorer Andressa Scabin became the first people to “GPS (global positioning system) tag” an arapaima, the world’s largest scaled freshwater fish.
National Geographic Explorer Fernando Trujillo has surveyed 1,270 kilometres of rivers across four countries, calculating there to be 1,366 of the iconic pink Amazon river dolphins in the region.

National Geographic Explorer Thiago Silva is creating the first three-dimensional model of the river’s floodplains, with the aim of creating an interactive environment that people can visit and explore online for themselves.

text.value: The teams have not achieved these results alone; they have been collaborating with local and Indigenous people. As Campos-Silva says: “We believe that a sustainable future for the Amazon is only possible if we have local people at the centre of the discussion. We need to ask how we can engage in conservation practices that local people have been doing for thousands of years.” One of the core aims of the expedition was for the teams to work closely with indigenous people and they have done just that: local people have been providing information for Bernardino and National Geographic Explorer Margaret Owuor on the ecosystem services provided by the mangroves, helping Campos-Silva and Scabin to locate the animals they have been tagging, and guided Perry and Matthews to the peak of Nevado Ausangate.

The findings have global implications for the state of the rainforest, and the expedition is only halfway done. “The Perpetual Planet Amazon Expedition brought together people with different specialities, with different research areas, and that has made our trip so much richer,” says Bernardino. With support from the long-standing partnership between Rolex and National Geographic, the teams hope to combine their knowledge and expertise across all their different fields to uncover the intricate and intertwined ecosystems that keep the world’s largest freshwater basin alive. Alongside the team of scientists and experts, Peschak will continue to document the discoveries as they unfold and change the way we see the Amazon.
